description: Personal pronouns, demonstratives, and question words

items.notes: Formal/written. In spoken Finnish often shortened to 'mä' or 'mää'.
examples: Minä olen opiskelija. — I am a student.

items.notes: Informal singular 'you'. Spoken: 'sä'. Formal: use 'Te'.
examples: Sinä olet mukava. — You are nice.

items.notes: Gender-neutral! Finnish has no he/she distinction. In spoken Finnish: 'se' (it) is commonly used for people too.
examples: Hän on opettaja. — He/She is a teacher.

items.notes: Always lowercase in Finnish unless starting a sentence.
examples: Me asumme Helsingissä. — We live in Helsinki.

items.notes: Plural 'you'. When capitalized 'Te', it becomes formal singular 'you' (like French 'vous').
examples: Te olette tervetulleita! — You (all) are welcome!

items.notes: Only for people. 'Ne' is used for things/animals in standard Finnish.
examples: He puhuvat suomea. — They speak Finnish.

items.notes: For things and animals. In spoken Finnish also used for people instead of 'hän'.
examples: Se on kaunis päivä. — It is a beautiful day.

items.notes: For things and animals. In spoken Finnish, also used for people.
examples: Ne ovat pöydällä. — They (the things) are on the table.

items.notes: Demonstrative pronoun for something close. Plural: 'nämä'.
examples: Tämä on minun kirjani. — This is my book.

items.notes: For something a bit farther away but still visible. Plural: 'nuo'.
examples: Tuo on kaunis talo. — That is a beautiful house.

items.notes: Question word. 'Mikä' for things, 'kuka' for people.
examples: Mikä tämä on? — What is this?

items.notes: Question word for people. Plural: 'ketkä'.
examples: Kuka sinä olet? — Who are you?

items.notes: Inessive case of 'mikä'. Asking about location (being somewhere).
examples: Missä sinä asut? — Where do you live?

items.notes: Question word for time.
examples: Milloin juna lähtee? — When does the train leave?

items.notes: Question word for reason. Translative case of 'mikä'.
examples: Miksi olet surullinen? — Why are you sad?

items.notes: Question word for manner. Also 'kuinka'.
examples: Miten pääsen keskustaan? — How do I get to the city center?

items.notes: Genitive of 'minä'. Often used with possessive suffix: 'minun kirjani' (my book).
examples: Tämä on minun. — This is mine.

items.notes: Genitive of 'sinä'. Informal.
examples: Onko tämä sinun laukkusi? — Is this your bag?

items.notes: Genitive of 'hän'. Gender-neutral like 'hän'.
examples: Hänen nimensä on Matti. — His/Her name is Matti.

items.notes: Genitive of 'me'.
examples: Meidän talo on tuolla. — Our house is over there.

items.notes: From 'paljon' (much/a lot) + '-ko' (question particle).
examples: Paljonko tämä maksaa? — How much does this cost?

items.notes: Used when choosing between two options. For more than two, use 'mikä'.
examples: Kumpi on parempi? — Which (of the two) is better?

items.notes: Indefinite pronoun. Can mean 'every' (adjective) or 'everyone' (noun).
examples: Jokainen päivä on erilainen. — Every day is different.

items.notes: Negative pronoun, used with negative verb. In questions means 'anyone'.
examples: Kukaan ei tiedä. — Nobody knows.

items.notes: Indefinite pronoun for an unspecified person.
examples: Joku soitti sinulle. — Someone called you.
